explanation:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architecture style for building web APIs. It treats everything as a resource — users, orders, products — each identified by a URL. You interact with resources using standard HTTP methods: GET reads, POST creates, PUT/PATCH updates, DELETE removes. This uniformity makes REST APIs predictable and easy to consume.

Think of REST like a library catalog system. Each book has a unique call number (URL). You can look up a book (GET), add a new one (POST), update its record (PUT), or remove it (DELETE). The catalog doesn't care what language you speak — it responds in a standard format (JSON), just like REST APIs.
key_takeaway: REST maps CRUD operations to HTTP methods on resource URLs — this uniformity is what makes APIs predictable and self-documenting.
try_it: Add a `/books/search?q=` query parameter endpoint that filters books by title.

explanation:
HTTP methods are verbs that tell the server what action to perform. GET is safe (no side effects), PUT is idempotent (repeating it gives the same result), and POST is neither — each call may create a new resource. Understanding these semantics prevents bugs like accidental duplicate orders on page refresh.

Status codes are the server's response signal. 2xx means success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content). 4xx means client error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found). 5xx means server error (500 Internal Server Error). Using the right code helps clients handle responses correctly without reading the body.
key_takeaway: Use 2xx for success, 4xx for client mistakes, 5xx for server failures — the status code is the first thing clients check before reading the body.
try_it: Add a PATCH endpoint that returns 400 if the client tries to change the item's `id` field.

explanation:
Every API call follows a cycle: the client sends a request with a method, URL, headers, and body. The server processes it and returns a response with a status code, headers, and body. Headers carry metadata — Content-Type tells the format (application/json), Authorization carries the token, Accept tells what format the client wants back.

Understanding this cycle is critical for debugging. A request might fail because of a missing header, wrong Content-Type, or a CORS policy blocking the origin. Tools like `curl`, Postman, or browser DevTools let you inspect every piece of the request-response pair.
example:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, Response, Header
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from typing import Optional
import json
import time

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def log_request(request: Request, call_next):
    print(f"→ {request.method} {request.url}")
    print(f"  Headers: {dict(request.headers)}")
    start = time.time()
    response = await call_next(request)
    duration = time.time() - start
    response.headers["X-Response-Time"] = f"{duration:.3f}s"
    print(f"← {response.status_code} ({duration:.3f}s)")
    return response

@app.get("/echo")
def echo(
    accept_language: Optional[str] = Header(None),
    user_agent: Optional[str] = Header(None)
):
    return {
        "your_language": accept_language,
        "your_browser": user_agent,
        "message": "Headers received!"
    }

@app.post("/mirror")
def mirror(request_body: dict):
    return JSONResponse(
        content={"you_sent": request_body},
        status_code=200,
        headers={"X-Custom": "mirrored"}
    )

# Test with curl:
# curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/mirror -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"hello":"world"}'
```
key_takeaway: Every API interaction is a request-response pair — headers carry metadata, the body carries data, and middleware can intercept both sides.
try_it: Add a middleware that returns 415 Unsupported Media Type if a POST request doesn't have `Content-Type: application/json`.

explanation:
Authentication answers "who are you?" The simplest method is an API key — a secret string sent in a header. It works but offers no granularity (all-or-nothing access). OAuth2 is the industry standard: users authorize third-party apps without sharing passwords ("Login with Google"). The app receives an access token after the user approves.

JWT (JSON Web Token) is a self-contained token encoding the user's identity and permissions. The server signs it with a secret; clients send it in the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header. JWTs are stateless — the server doesn't store session data — but can't be revoked easily, so short expiration times are essential.
key_takeaway: API keys are simple but coarse; OAuth2 delegates auth to identity providers; JWTs are self-verifying tokens — use short expiry and never store secrets in them.
try_it: Add a `require_admin` dependency that decodes the JWT and returns 403 if `role != "admin"`.

explanation:
Without rate limiting, a single client can overwhelm your API with thousands of requests per second, degrading service for everyone. Rate limiting caps how many requests a client can make in a time window (e.g., 100 requests per minute). Exceeding the limit returns 429 Too Many Requests, and response headers (`X-RateLimit-Remaining`) tell clients how much quota they have left.

Pagination prevents a single request from returning millions of records. Offset-based pagination uses `?page=2&limit=20` but can miss items if data changes between pages. Cursor-based pagination uses a pointer to the last item (`?after=abc123`) — more reliable for large, changing datasets. Always set a maximum page size to protect your database.
example:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query, HTTPException, Request
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from collections import defaultdict
import time

app = FastAPI()

# In-memory rate limiter
rate_limit_store: dict[str, list[float]] = defaultdict(list)
RATE_LIMIT = 10  # requests per window
WINDOW = 60      # seconds

@app.middleware("http")
async def rate_limit(request: Request, call_next):
    client_ip = request.client.host
    now = time.time()
    rate_limit_store[client_ip] = [
        t for t in rate_limit_store[client_ip] if now - t < WINDOW
    ]
    if len(rate_limit_store[client_ip]) >= RATE_LIMIT:
        return JSONResponse(
            status_code=429,
            content={"detail": "Rate limit exceeded"},
            headers={"Retry-After": str(WINDOW)}
        )
    rate_limit_store[client_ip].append(now)
    response = await call_next(request)
    remaining = RATE_LIMIT - len(rate_limit_store[client_ip])
    response.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"] = str(remaining)
    return response

# Cursor-based pagination
data = [{"id": i, "name": f"Item {i}"} for i in range(1, 101)]

@app.get("/items")
def list_items(
    cursor: int = Query(0, ge=0),
    limit: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=100)
):
    start = cursor
    end = start + limit
    items = data[start:end]
    next_cursor = end if end < len(data) else None
    return {
        "items": items,
        "next_cursor": next_cursor,
        "has_more": next_cursor is not None
    }
```
key_takeaway: Rate limiting protects your API from abuse; pagination protects your database from runaway queries — both are non-negotiable in production.
try_it: Change the rate limiter to use a token bucket algorithm instead of a fixed window.

explanation:
Good API design is invisible — developers intuitively know how to use it. Use nouns for resources (`/users`, not `/getUsers`), plural names consistently, and nested resources for relationships (`/users/123/orders`). Version your API from day one (`/v1/users`) so breaking changes don't break clients. Keep request/response shapes consistent across endpoints.

Error responses should follow a predictable structure: include an error code, a human-readable message, and optional details. Use HATEOAS links sparingly — most REST APIs don't need them. Document your conventions in a style guide so all endpoints feel like they were designed by one person.
key_takeaway: Use nouns for resources, plural names, versioned URLs, and consistent response shapes — if every endpoint feels different, your API is badly designed.
try_it: Add a PATCH endpoint for updating order status with validation that only allows valid transitions (pending → shipped → delivered).

practice_challenges.title: RESTful Resource Design
practice_challenges.hint: Map CRUD operations to POST/GET/PUT/DELETE with proper status codes
practice_challenges.description: Design and implement a RESTful API for a library system with /books, /authors, and /borrowers resources. Each endpoint must use appropriate HTTP methods, return correct status codes (201 for creation, 204 for deletion), and support pagination.

practice_challenges.title: API Rate Limiting Middleware
practice_challenges.hint: Use a sliding window counter stored in Redis or in-memory
practice_challenges.description: Implement a rate limiting middleware that allows 100 requests per minute per API key. Return proper 429 Too Many Requests responses with Retry-After headers. Support both per-user and global rate limits.
practice_challenges.example_input: API key abc123 makes 101st request in 60 seconds

practice_challenges.title: API Versioning Strategy
practice_challenges.hint: Use URL path versioning (/v1/, /v2/) with a deprecation header for old versions
practice_challenges.description: Design an API versioning system that supports v1 and v2 simultaneously. V2 adds a metadata wrapper around responses. Implement a middleware that routes to the correct version handler and adds deprecation headers for v1 endpoints.

practice_challenges.title: GraphQL Schema with Pagination
practice_challenges.hint: Use relay-style cursor pagination with first, after, hasNextPage
practice_challenges.description: Implement a GraphQL schema for an e-commerce platform with products and categories. Support cursor-based pagination (Relay specification) for product listings. Include filtering by category, price range, and availability.
practice_challenges.example_input: query { products(first: 10, after: cursor123, filter: {category: electronics}) }
practice_challenges.example_output: Connection with edges, pageInfo { hasNextPage, endCursor }, and filtered products

practice_challenges.title: Webhook Delivery System with Retry
practice_challenges.hint: Use a message queue with dead-letter handling and exponential backoff retries
practice_challenges.description: Build a webhook delivery system that sends HTTP POST requests to registered endpoints when events occur. Implement exponential backoff retry (up to 5 attempts), dead-letter queue for permanently failed deliveries, and signature verification using HMAC-SHA256.
practice_challenges.example_input: Event: order.created | Webhook URL: https://client.com/webhooks | Payload: {order_id: 42}
practice_challenges.example_output: Delivery with X-Webhook-Signature header; retry at 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s intervals

explanation:
An undocumented API is a product nobody can use. OpenAPI (formerly Swagger) is the industry standard for describing REST APIs in a machine-readable YAML/JSON format. It defines every endpoint, its parameters, request/response schemas, authentication methods, and error codes. Tools generate interactive docs, client SDKs, and test suites from this single source of truth.

FastAPI auto-generates OpenAPI specs from your code — the type hints, Pydantic models, and decorators become the documentation. Visit `/docs` for Swagger UI (interactive try-it-out panels) or `/redoc` for ReDoc (clean reference docs). This means your docs are always in sync with your code — no manual updating required.
example:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query, Path
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional
from datetime import datetime

app = FastAPI(
    title="Task Manager API",
    description="A simple task management API built with FastAPI.",
    version="1.0.0",
    contact={"name": "Dev Team", "email": "api@example.com"},
    license_info={"name": "MIT"}
)

class TaskCreate(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=200, description="Task title")
    priority: str = Field("medium", pattern="^(low|medium|high)$", description="Priority level")
    due_date: Optional[datetime] = Field(None, description="Optional deadline")

class TaskResponse(BaseModel):
    id: int
    title: str
    priority: str
    completed: bool
    created_at: datetime

tasks_db: dict[int, dict] = {}

@app.post(
    "/tasks",
    response_model=TaskResponse,
    status_code=201,
    summary="Create a new task",
    description="Creates a task with a title, optional priority, and due date.",
    tags=["tasks"]
)
def create_task(task: TaskCreate):
    """Create a new task in the system."""
    task_id = len(tasks_db) + 1
    record = {
        "id": task_id,
        "title": task.title,
        "priority": task.priority,
        "completed": False,
        "created_at": datetime.now()
    }
    tasks_db[task_id] = record
    return record

@app.get(
    "/tasks/{task_id}",
    response_model=TaskResponse,
    summary="Get a task by ID",
    tags=["tasks"]
)
def get_task(
    task_id: int = Path(..., ge=1, description="The task ID")
):
    """Retrieve a single task by its ID."""
    if task_id not in tasks_db:
        from fastapi import HTTPException
        raise HTTPException(404, "Task not found")
    return tasks_db[task_id]

@app.get("/tasks", response_model=list[TaskResponse], tags=["tasks"])
def list_tasks(
    completed: Optional[bool] = Query(None, description="Filter by status"),
    limit: int = Query(50, ge=1, le=200)
):
    """List all tasks with optional filters."""
    results = list(tasks_db.values())
    if completed is not None:
        results = [t for t in results if t["completed"] == completed]
    return results[:limit]

# Visit http://localhost:8000/docs for Swagger UI
# Visit http://localhost:8000/redoc for ReDoc
# Visit http://localhost:8000/openapi.json for raw spec
```
key_takeaway: FastAPI auto-generates OpenAPI docs from your type hints — your docs are always in sync because they ARE your code. Visit /docs to see the magic.
try_it: Add `tags=["admin"]` and a `deprecated=True` parameter to an endpoint, then check how it appears in the Swagger UI at `/docs`.

explanation:
API interview problems test your ability to design, implement, and secure RESTful endpoints. The key patterns are: resource-oriented URL design, proper HTTP method and status code usage, authentication with JWT/OAuth2, pagination strategies, and rate limiting. Understanding these patterns separates developers who build usable APIs from those who just make endpoints work.

The most important skill is designing APIs that feel consistent across every endpoint. If one endpoint returns {data: [...]} and another returns {results: [...]}, developers will struggle. Pick conventions (naming, response shape, error format) and enforce them everywhere. Practice by designing APIs for real-world scenarios: e-commerce, social media, project management.
key_takeaway: Design APIs with consistent resource naming, proper HTTP status codes, and standardized response shapes across every endpoint.
try_it: Design a complete REST API for a library system with books, authors, and borrowing records.

practice_challenges.title: Design a User Registration API
practice_challenges.hint: Use POST for creation, 201 Created on success, 409 Conflict for duplicate emails, and validate input with Pydantic.
practice_challenges.description: Build a user registration endpoint that validates email format, enforces unique emails, hashes passwords before storage, and returns proper HTTP status codes for each case.

practice_challenges.title: Implement Cursor-Based Pagination
practice_challenges.hint: Return a cursor (encoded ID or timestamp) with each page. The client passes the cursor to get the next page. Include hasMore boolean.
practice_challenges.description: Add cursor-based pagination to a products endpoint. Support limit parameter and return products sorted by creation date with a next_cursor for fetching the next page.

practice_challenges.title: Build an API with JWT Authentication
practice_challenges.hint: Implement login endpoint that returns JWT, then protect other endpoints with a dependency that decodes and validates the token.
practice_challenges.description: Create a login endpoint returning JWT tokens, protect a /profile endpoint that requires authentication, and implement role-based access where only admins can access /admin/users.
practice_challenges.example_input: POST /login {"username": "admin", "password": "pass"} then GET /profile with Bearer token
practice_challenges.example_output: Login returns {"access_token": "eyJ...", "token_type": "bearer"}. Profile returns user data. Admin-only returns 403 for non-admins.

practice_challenges.title: Implement Rate Limiting with Sliding Window
practice_challenges.hint: Use a sliding window algorithm: store timestamps of requests per client, remove expired entries, and reject when count exceeds the limit.
practice_challenges.description: Build middleware that limits each client to 100 requests per minute using a sliding window algorithm. Return 429 status with Retry-After header when exceeded, and include X-RateLimit-Remaining in responses.
practice_challenges.example_output: First 100: 200 OK with X-RateLimit-Remaining header. 101st: 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After: 60

practice_challenges.title: Design a REST API for an E-Commerce Platform
practice_challenges.hint: Think about resource hierarchies: /users/{id}/orders, /orders/{id}/items. Include proper status codes for each operation, nested filtering, and consistent error responses.
practice_challenges.description: Design and implement a complete e-commerce API with users, products, shopping carts, and orders. Support nested resources, proper HTTP methods, versioned URLs (/v1/), pagination, and consistent error response format across all endpoints.
practice_challenges.example_output: Cart adds item with 200. Order creation returns 201 with order details including calculated total from cart items.
